content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission,
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

May 24, 1976

Paul B. Wells
Procopio, Cory, Hargreaves
and Savitch
1900 California First Bank
Building
530 B Street
San Diego, California 92101

Dear Mr. Wells:

Your letter of May 5, 1976, to John Greenwood has been
referred to me for a response. On the basis of facts stated
in that letter, you have correctly concluded that the San Diego
Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) will be a "committee"
pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(c) if it makes
monetary contributions totalling $5,000 or more in a calendar
year. As long as CIAF engages in no independent campaign activity
and makes no expenditures on non-monetary contributions it is not
a "committee" as defined in Government Code Section 82013(b).
Moreover, CIAF is not a committee within the meaning of Government
Code Section 82013(a) because the funds it receives are not
"contributions". You stated that in 1973, CIAF made no reportable
contributions. In 1974, it contributed 4.4% of its income to
committees supporting or opposing ballot measures and in 1975
made no reportable contributions. Based on this prior course of
conduct as well as the arrangement under which individual contractors
make payments to the fund, you have concluded correctly that the
funds received should not be characterized as "contributions".

This informal opinion is based only on the facts stated in
your letter and would not be applicable if CIAF changes its
procedures and begins to engage in political activities on a
regular basis so that the contractor has reason to know that a
portion of his payments will be used to make contributions or
expenditures.

I hope this information will be of assistance.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West
Staff Counsel

ROBERT G. RUSSELL, JR.

LAW OFFICES OF

PROCOPIO, CORY, HARGREAVES AND SAVITCH
1900 CALIFORNIA FIRST BANK BUILDING
630 B STREET
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101

A. T. PROCOPIO
1900-1974

AREA CODE 714
TELEPHONE 238-1900

May 6, 1976

Mr. John C. Greenwood
Program Coordinator
Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: San Diego Construction Industry
    Advancement Fund - Reporting Duties
    Pursuant to Political Reform Act of 1974

Dear Mr. Greenwood:

     Enclosed is the San Diego Construction Industry
Advancement Fund Trust Agreement which was inadvertently
omitted in my letter to you of May 5, 1976. I am also
enclosing a copy of my May 5 letter for your reference.

     My apologies for any inconvenience this oversight
may have caused you.

                                        Yours very truly,

                                        [signature]

                                        PAUL B. WELLS of
                                        Procopio, Cory, Hargreaves
                                        and Savitch

Enclosure

LAW OFFICES OF

ALEC L.CORY
HARRY J. HARGREAVES
EMMANUEL SAVITCH
JOHN H. BARKLEY
PAUL B. WELLS
RONALD P. BURKE
RICHARD L. ROBINSON
TODD E. LEIGH
JEFFREY ISAACS
LAWRENCE ASHER
ROBERT JAMES BERTON
RICHARD B. MUNKS
STANLEY J. SCOTT
JOHN C. MALUGEN
FREDERICK M. KUNZEL
ROBERT G. RUSSELL,JR.

PROCOPIO, CORY, HARGREAVES AND SAVITCH
[illegible] CALIFORNIA FIRST BANK BUILDING
530 B STREET

SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101

AREA CODE 714
TELEPHONE 238-1900

A. T. PROCOPIO
1900-1974

June 25, 1976

[illegible]

Natalie E. West, Esquire
State of California Fair
Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: San Diego Construction Industry
Advancement Fund

Dear Ms. West:

Thank you for your letter of June 22, 1976 requesting
a clarification of my letter to the Fair Political Practices
Commission of May 5, 1976, with respect to whether our client,
San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund, makes con-
tributions to campaigns involving candidates for elective
office.

From the very inception of the San Diego Construction
Industry Advancement Fund there has been a firm written policy
that no grants will be used to in any way support or oppose
any candidate for any political office, federal, state or
local. This policy is and has been strictly adhered to and
will continue to be in the future.

If you should have any other questions, please advise.

Sincerely yours,

[illegible]

PAUL B. WELLS of
Procopio, Cory, Hargreaves
and Savitch

PBW:bw

cc: Mr. Stanley Scott
San Diego Construction
Industry Advancement Fund

May 5, 1976


Mr. John C. Greenwood
Program Coordinator
Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: San Diego Construction Industry Advancement
Fund - Reporting Duties Pursuant to Political
Reform Act of 1974

Dear Mr. Greenwood:

Pursuant to your suggestion to Mr. Fiorello, a law
clerk with our firm, this is to request an informal opinion
from you concerning the reporting duties of our client, San
Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund,1/ (hereafter
"CIAF"), under the Political Reform Act of 1974 (hereafter
"the Act").

The CIAF is a non-profit trust and is exempt from
both federal and state income tax reporting requirements.
It was established in 1972 as a part of the collective
bargaining process between three employer associations, the
Associated General Contractors, San Diego Chapter, Inc.
("AGC"), the San Diego Building Contractors Association
("BCA") and the Engineering and Grading Contractors Association, Inc.2/ ("EGCA") and the five major local construction unions, the Laborers Union, Carpenters Union,

_________________________________________________________________

1/ Due to the collective bargaining process, there are
actually two trusts, San Diego Construction Industry
Advancement Fund and San Diego Construction Industry
Advancement Fund (OE). The San Diego Construction Industry
Advancement Fund (OE) receives funds based upon hours
worked by members of the Operating Engineers Union. The
other Fund receives monies based upon hours worked by members of four other basic construction industry unions. Both
funds are controlled by the identical trustees and are
managed, in effect, as a single trust.

2/ This name was later changed to Engineering and General
Contractors Association, Inc.

Mr. John C. Greenwood
May 5, 1976
Page Two


Construction Teamsters Union, Cement Masons Union and
Operating Engineers Union. The three employer associa-
tions bargain on behalf of most of the construction con-
tractors in San Diego County.

Under the labor agreements each contractor which
employs members of any of the five basic construction
unions is required to pay a fixed sum per man-hour worked
to the CIAP. An example of the contract clause requiring
such payments is:

"For the purposes of protecting and
promoting the interests and welfare of the
construction industry, its individual EMPLOYERS
and employees, the parties hereto agree to the
establishment of a CONSTRUCTION INDUSTRY ADVANCEMENT
FUND which shall comply with all applicable
laws and regulations and which shall be based
upon an EMPLOYER contribution of two cents (2c)
per hour for each hour paid for Carpenters,
effective May 15, 1974, and increasing to five
cents (5c) per hour for each hour for Carpenters
effective July 1, 1974."
(Master Labor Agreement for San Diego County
Covering Construction Work by Carpenters,
June 16, 1974 - June 15, 1977, section 50.)

This sum was initially two cents ($0.02) per hour and is
now generally five cents ($0.05) per hour. The payments
are typically made on a monthly basis to the administrators
for the various labor union health and welfare, vacation
and pension trusts and forwarded to the CIAP by those
administrators.3/


3/ On a periodic basis, usually monthly, each employer remits
to each union's trust administrator the total amount of fringe
benefit payments earned by members of that union. For con-
venience, each employer includes its payment to CIAP in that
total amount. The administrator then pays to the CIAP the
amount due. The CIAP is never currently apprised of the
identity of the employers who contribute, since those records
remain with the trust administrators. Annually, the trust
administrators furnish the CIAP with the names and addresses
of all employers who made payments during the previous year.

Mr. John C. Greenwood
May 5, 1976
Page Three


     The CIAF receives funds indirectly from every con-
tractor which employs members of the basic construction
unions in San Diego County. This includes not only con-
tractors resident in the county but also contractors from
other areas of the state and the United States who engage
in construction in this county, often on only a single
project or other short-term basis. Such non-resident
contractors are subject to the terms of the San Diego
County collective bargaining agreements during their work
in the county.

     As is evident, the number and identity of contributors
to the CIAF and the amount of contributions may and do vary
substantially from month to month, depending upon the amount
of work in progress, the number of contractors engaged in
local construction, the severity of local weather conditions
and the myriad other variables inherent in the industry.
The CIAF has no way of knowing from whom it will receive
funds or the amount of those funds from month to month. For
example, in 1975, contributions ranged from a low of
$40,110.78 in March to a high of $54,461.10 in June. The
total number of contributors in 1975 exceeded 2400.

     The management of the CIAF is vested in its Board of
Trustees. The Board is comprised of seven (7) persons, two
appointed by each of the three employer associations and one
appointed from the public at large by the other six trustees.
None of the trustees receives any compensation for his ser-
vice on the Board.

     Other than continuing overhead (office rental, supplies,
staff and the like), all expenditures by CIAF are the results
of approved applications for grants. Grants may be requested
by any person or organization and must be requested in writing.
Grant applications are considered by the Board of Trustees in
light of many factors, including the availability of funds,
similarity to other projects funded by the CIAF, legality of
purpose and the proposed method of its accomplishment. The
single most important factor, however, is how closely the
objective of the grant is related to the stated purposes of
the CIAF, which are set out in Sections 1.1 through 1.4 of
the enclosed copy of the Trust Agreement4/. No contributing

_________________________________________________________________

4/ The Trust Agreement has been amended from time to time since
   this booklet was published. None of the amendments has any
   effect upon Sections 1.1-1.4 or this opinion request.

Mr. John C. Greenwood
May 5, 1976
Page Four


employer has any right to control the expenditure of any
of the funds received by the CIAF, e.g., Section 6.3 of
the Trust Agreement.

Grant applications are considered by the Board of
Trustees at its monthly meeting and are approved or re-
jected on the basis of factors existing at that time. No
general budget for the granting of funds to various cate-
gories is established on a yearly or other periodic basis,
due in great part to the unpredictability of the number and
type of grant applications and the receipt of funds. That
is, there is no attempt to program on a yearly or other
periodic basis the amounts of money or percentages of re-
ceipts which will or may be granted for use in such general
areas as education and training, public relations, safety,
ballot measure support or opposition, research and other
categories for which grants have been made in the past and
may be sought in the future.

The CIAF has in the past granted funds to applicants
supporting and opposing various ballot measures affecting
the construction industry, following the procedure set out
above. It may or may not do so in the future, again follow-
ing the procedures set out above. At no time is it possible
to state what amounts of money or percentage of receipts, if
any, will be devoted, directly or indirectly, to purposes con-
cerning which reports of one kind or another may be required
by the Act. For example, in 1973 the CIAF made no reportable
contributions. In 1974, it contributed 4.4% of its income to
committees supporting or opposing various ballot measures and
in 1975 it made no such contributions.

We have advised the CIAF that it will be a "committee"
pursuant to section 82013(c) of the Act at such times as it
makes contributions in the total amount of $5,000.00 or more
in a calendar year and that it will be subject to the report-
ing requirements of such a committee.

We have further advised the CIAF that, in our opinion,
it is not a "committee" as defined in either section 82103(a)
or 82103(b). With respect to section 82103(b), the CIAF
makes no contributions other than contributions described
in section 82013(c) and, consequently, it could not be an
82013(b) committee.

Mr. John C. Greenwood
May 5, 1976
Page Five


     With respect to section 82013(a), the CIAF does not,
in our opinion, receive "contributions" as defined in the
Act, section 82015. A "contribution" is "a payment...unless
it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it was
not made for political purposes."

     On April 22, 1976, the Commission adopted a Regulation
with respect to contributions. (2 Cal.Admin. Code 18213,
hereafter "Reg. 18215".) This regulation provides that

     "'Contribution' includes any monetary or non-
monetary payment...that is received by or made
at behest of...[a]n organization formed or
existing primarily for political purposes....."

(Reg. 18214(b)(4) emphasis added.) The enclosed Trust Agreement
reveals that the CIAF exists for numerous purposes, among
the least of which is political. If, among its wide array of
grants, the CIAF occasionally contributes to the support or
opposition of a ballot measure, this activity certainly does
not conclude it is an organization existing primarily for
political purposes.

     Moreover, the Commission's recent regulation on contri-
butions also provides, in pertinent part:

     "Any monetary...payment...received by any...
organization...is a contribution if the payment
is 'earmarked' for the making of contributions
or expenditures. A payment is 'earmarked' when,
at the time of making the payment, the donor
knows or has reason to know that the payment or
funds with which the payment will be commingled
will be used to make contributions....."

(Reg. 18214(d), emphasis added.)

     In addition, the Regulation provides:

     "Factors relevant to a determination that the
donor has reason to know that all or part of
his payment will be used to make expenditures
and contributions include but are not limited
to the established practice of the...organiza-
tion with respect to...contributions and any
representations made when the payment is solicited."

(Id., emphasis added.) First, payments to the CIAF are not
earmarked for either political contributions or expenditures.

Mr. John C. Greenwood
May 5, 1976
Page Six


Second, the CIAF's established practice as set forth above
shows that the contractors have no reason to know that any
part of the funds will be used for political purposes. Third,
at the time a contractor becomes bound to the collective bar-
gaining agreements and thereafter, the CIAF makes no repre-
sentations that the payments will be used for any particular
purposes, political or otherwise. Finally, the contractors'
payments are not solicited but are required by labor agree-
ments.

It seems inarguable that the individual contractors
which make payments to the fund administrators for the CIAF
do not make them "for political purposes". The contractors
make the payments because they are required to do so by a
collective bargaining agreement with which they must comply
in order to engage in their business. They are subject to
civil liability for failure to make the payments. They
have no choice in the matter nor have they any right to
direct or control the disposition of the funds received by
the CIAF. Indeed, they have no knowledge of how the funds
are specifically expended, absent inquiry after the fact.
The payments are in no way similar to "dues" paid by members
of voluntary organizations, as those members may decide not
to pay their dues or to withdraw from the organization without
cutting injuring their businesses or incurring civil liability.
The payments are far more similar to "dues" paid by members
of labor unions as a condition to retaining their jobs.

In light of the circumstances set out above, it seems
clear that the CIAF is not a "committee" as defined in
section 82103(a) of the Act and is not required to file a
Committee Campaign Statement, form 420.

The CIAF has no desire to withhold any information con-
cerning itself, its activities or its sources of funds. Its
books and records are open to any person or agency with a
legitimate interest in them. It has filed and will continue
to file all reports required of a major donor when it comes
within that category.

On the other hand, the proper preparation of a form 420
Schedule A (List of Contributors with "Amount Received" and
"Cumulative Amount") would be almost if not completely impos-
sible and would, in any event, be a staggering burden. As
stated above, there were some 2400 separate employers who
made payments to the CIAF through the various trust admini-
strators during 1975. Many made payments each month, others
undoubtedly made as few as one payment.

John C. Greenwood
May 5, 1976
Page Seven


     The CIAF has no knowledge of the date or dates upon
which a particular employer makes a payment which ulti-
mately flows to the CIAF.  It is no doubt the case that
some employer will make its first payment in November. If
the CIAF had contributed to a ballot measure committee in
the preceding April, no one could argue that the employer
making the November payment had made a "contribution".

     While we believe it is clear that the CIAF is not a
section 82013(a) committee, in light of the very heavy
penalties both criminal and civil which may be imposed for
violation of the Act, it seems prudent to seek your con-
currence in this matter.

     I will certainly appreciate your attention to this
important matter and will be happy to provide any further
information which you may desire.

                                        Yours very truly,


                                        PAUL B. WELLS of
                                        Procopio, Cory, Hargreaves
                                               and Savitch

PBW:bw

CC:  SDCIAF

sections.question: What are the reporting duties of the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) under the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically whether CIAF constitutes a 'committee' requiring disclosure and reporting?
sections.conclusion: On the basis of facts stated in that letter, you have correctly concluded that the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) will be a 'committee' pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(c) if it makes monetary contributions totalling $5,000 or more in a calendar year. As long as CIAF engages in no independent campaign activity and makes no expenditures or non-monetary contributions it is not a 'committee' as defined in Government Code Section 82013(b). Moreover, CIAF is not a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(a) because the funds it receives are not 'contributions'. Based on the prior course of conduct and the arrangement under which individual contractors make payments to the fund, the funds received should not be characterized as 'contributions'.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund, a non-profit trust established through collective bargaining that receives per-hour contributions from construction contractors and makes grants to various causes including some ballot measure campaigns, qualifies as a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act of 1974 requiring disclosure and reporting.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: CIAF will be a committee only if it makes monetary contributions of $5,000 or more in a calendar year under Section 82013(c). The funds CIAF receives are not 'contributions' under the Act because they are mandatory per-hour payments under collective bargaining agreements rather than voluntary political contributions, and CIAF is not a committee under sections 82013(a) or (b) as long as it engages in no independent campaign activity.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains FPPC response letter dated May 24, 1976, plus supporting correspondence from the requesting law firm. The response is clear with explicit Q/A structure. Includes follow-up correspondence clarifying candidate support policy. Minor OCR issues but core content extracted reliably.

QUESTION: What are the reporting duties of the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) under the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically whether CIAF constitutes a 'committee' requiring disclosure and reporting?

CONCLUSION: On the basis of facts stated in that letter, you have correctly concluded that the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF) will be a 'committee' pursuant to Government Code Section 82013(c) if it makes monetary contributions totalling $5,000 or more in a calendar year. As long as CIAF engages in no independent campaign activity and makes no expenditures or non-monetary contributions it is not a 'committee' as defined in Government Code Section 82013(b). Moreover, CIAF is not a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(a) because the funds it receives are not 'contributions'. Based on the prior course of conduct and the arrangement under which individual contractors make payments to the fund, the funds received should not be characterized as 'contributions'.

As long as CIAF engages in no independent campaign activity and makes no expenditures on non-monetary contributions it is not a "committee" as defined in Government Code Section 82013(b). Moreover, CIAF is not a committee within the meaning of Government Code Section 82013(a) because the funds it receives are not "contributions". You stated that in 1973, CIAF made no reportable contributions. embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that the San Diego Construction Industry Advancement Fund (CIAF), a non-profit trust receiving mandatory per-hour contributions from construction contractors under collective bargaining agreements, will qualify as a 'committee' only if it makes monetary contributions totaling $5,000 or more in a calendar year, and that the funds it receives are not 'contributions' under the Political Reform Act.
